summary: Fowler addresses the common complaint that agile methods lack rigorous definition, making it impossible to verify if teams are truly practicing agile or to create standardized curricula. He argues this lack of rigor is not a flaw but an essential feature of agile philosophy. Because software development contexts vary enormously and are fundamentally human-oriented, agile methods expect teams to adapt and regularly change their processes. This makes formal conformance testing impossible by design, which Fowler acknowledges is frustrating but unavoidable—like getting wet while swimming.
key_points: The lack of rigorous definition in agile is intentional, not a deficiency—it reflects agile's core philosophy
Software development's varied contexts and human-centric nature make prescriptive processes ineffective
Agile expects teams to decide and continuously evolve their own processes, making conformance testing contradictory to agile principles
Determining whether a team is 'really doing agile' requires experienced practitioners and still invites disagreement
Our profession's comfort with programming computers leads us to mistakenly try programming human systems the same way
money_quotes: This lack of rigorousness is part of the defining nature of agile methods, part of its core philosophy.
It's not effective to try and bind software development to a rigorous process, because that's ignoring the essential nature of the primary (human) components that will execute that process.
It's probably because our profession is to work with computers is what leads us to want to program human systems the same way that we program computers.
Agile methods fundamentally expect teams to decide what process to follow and furthermore expect teams to actively and regularly change their process.
Any attempt to define a rigorous process that can be tested for conformance runs contrary to this philosophy.
There is a sense of 'I know it when I see it', but it requires an experienced practitioner to have that sense.
It's an unfortunate consequence of the activity itself, just as getting wet is an unavoidable consequence of swimming.
key_insight: Agile's lack of rigorous definition is not a bug to be fixed but an essential feature that acknowledges the irreducibly human and variable nature of software development.
